title: When should I eat breakfast?
answerTemplate: Eat breakfast {hoursSinceWake}–2 hours after waking ({optimalBreakfastTime}). Your wake time is {wakeTime}, so aim for {optimalBreakfastTime} to align with cortisol rhythm.

ifThenTemplate.then: Wait 30min, have water, reassess — forced eating disrupts leptin

whyTemplate: Cortisol peaks 30-60min post-wake. Eating too early blunts natural awakening cascade; waiting allows proper metabolic priming.

title: When should I eat lunch?
answerTemplate: Eat lunch 4-6 hours after breakfast, around {optimalLunchTime}. Aim for 12:00-1:00pm window for peak digestive enzyme activity.

ifThenTemplate.then: Shift ±30min max — beyond that, insulin timing suffers

whyTemplate: Digestive enzymes peak midday. Consistent timing trains circadian metabolic rhythms for optimal glucose clearance.

title: When should I eat dinner?
answerTemplate: Eat dinner 3+ hours before bed ({dinnerDeadline}). Your bedtime is {bedtime}, so finish eating by {dinnerDeadline} to preserve sleep quality.

ifThenTemplate.then: Cut portion by 30% and keep it protein-focused — late carbs spike insulin overnight

whyTemplate: Late meals disrupt REM sleep and morning insulin sensitivity. The 3-hour buffer allows digestion to complete before sleep.

title: Can I snack between meals?
answerTemplate: On {dayMode} mode: {snackRule}. Last meal was {hoursSinceLastMeal}h ago. If true hunger (not habit), wait until 3h post-meal minimum.

nextMovesTemplate.title: Assess: stomach emptiness or mouth boredom?
If true hunger: 20g protein snack only

ifThenTemplate.then: Wait — snacking now ruins meal appetite and doubles insulin exposure
Increase meal 1 & 2 portions by 15% — persistent hunger signals under-eating

whyTemplate: Frequent eating keeps insulin elevated, blocking fat oxidation. Meal spacing preserves metabolic flexibility.

title: When can I have treats like candy or dessert?
answerTemplate: Optimal treat window: 2-3h after protein meal, at least 2h before bed. Next window: {treatWindowStart}–{treatWindowEnd}.

ifThenTemplate.then: Drink 16oz water, wait 15min — often thirst/habit, not need
Risk blood sugar crash within 90min — protein first always

whyTemplate: Treats spike glucose and insulin. Timing after protein + distance from sleep minimizes metabolic disruption and preserves REM quality.

title: When should I have coffee?
answerTemplate: First coffee: 90min after waking ({firstCaffeineTime}). Cutoff: 8h before bed ({caffeineCutoff}). Current time: {nowLocal} — {caffeineAdvice}.

ifThenTemplate.then: 10min walk + cold water — not more caffeine. Half-life is 5-6h

whyTemplate: Cortisol is naturally high on waking. Caffeine then creates tolerance. The 8h cutoff preserves deep sleep architecture.

title: Why am I tired at 3pm?
answerTemplate: Natural circadian dip 7-9h post-wake (2-4pm). Sleep quality last night: {sleepQuality}/10. If <7, sleep debt is the root cause, not food.

nextMovesTemplate.title: Return to work — dip passes naturally

ifThenTemplate.then: Check: sleep debt, meal timing, dehydration. Dip is normal; collapse is not

whyTemplate: Your suprachiasmatic nucleus creates an alertness dip 7-9h post-wake. Movement and light reset the system without caffeine dependency.

title: What time should I sleep?
answerTemplate: Target bedtime: {bedtime} (your setting). Winddown starts 90min before ({winddownStart}). Sleep quality last night: {sleepQuality}/10.

ifThenTemplate.then: Go to another room, read under dim light — don't force it in bed

whyTemplate: Consistent sleep timing trains circadian rhythm. The 90min winddown allows adenosine buildup and melatonin release to sync.

title: How do I manage stress?
answerTemplate: Current stress level: {stressLevel}/10. If >6, prioritize: sleep debt recovery, meal timing consistency, daily movement. No amount of optimization works on chronic stress + poor sleep.

nextMovesTemplate.title: Assess sleep: aim for 7.5h minimum tonight

ifThenTemplate.then: Schedule professional help — behavioral OS won't fix structural life issues

whyTemplate: Stress dysregulates cortisol, sleep, and eating behavior. You can't optimize your way out of chronic stress.

title: Best time to workout?
answerTemplate: Best windows: Morning (+2h post-wake, {morningWorkout}) for fat oxidation, or afternoon (+8h, {afternoonWorkout}) for strength. Avoid evening if sleep is <7.

nextMovesTemplate.title: Rest day tomorrow if sleep <7h tonight

ifThenTemplate.then: Keep it <45min — glycogen depletes fast, form suffers

whyTemplate: Morning: cortisol peak aids fat oxidation. Afternoon: body temp + muscle activation peak. Evening workouts disrupt sleep if within 3h of bed.

title: How long should I fast?
answerTemplate: Current target: {fastingHours}h. On {dayMode} mode, aim for {fastingHours}h minimum. Last meal: {lastMealTime} ({hoursSinceLastMeal}h ago).

ifThenTemplate.then: Small protein snack OK — better than binge that breaks containment

whyTemplate: Fasting windows train metabolic flexibility and insulin sensitivity. Consistency matters more than perfection.

title: Can I shift my meal times?
answerTemplate: You can shift ±30min without major impact. Beyond that, insulin timing and circadian alignment suffer. Next meal: {nextMealTime}.

ifThenTemplate.then: Accept it, don't force eating to 'catch up' — resume schedule next meal

whyTemplate: Small shifts are fine. Large disruptions signal you need to redesign your schedule, not fight it daily.

title: When should I drink water?
answerTemplate: Front-load water: 16oz on waking, 8oz before each meal. Taper after 6pm to avoid sleep disruption. Current time: {nowLocal}.
whyTemplate: Morning hydration kickstarts metabolism. Pre-meal water improves satiety. Evening taper protects sleep continuity.

title: When should I walk?
answerTemplate: Best: 10-15min after each meal (glucose disposal). Also: morning sunlight walk, evening winddown walk. Next meal: {nextMealTime}.
whyTemplate: Post-meal walks improve glucose clearance. Morning light entrains circadian clock. Evening walks aid sleep onset.

title: How much should I eat per meal?
answerTemplate: Aim for satiety, not fullness. Protein-first plate (palm-sized protein, fist of veg, cupped hand of carbs). Adjust by hunger patterns between meals.

nextMovesTemplate.title: Plate: palm protein, fist veg, cupped carbs
Assess hunger 3h later
nextMovesTemplate.reason: Too little = hungry early; too much = sluggish

ifThenTemplate.then: Increase protein by 20% next time

whyTemplate: Portion control isn't about calories — it's about training satiety signals and metabolic flexibility.

title: Can I skip breakfast?
answerTemplate: Yes, if it extends fasting to {fastingHours}h+ and you're not hungry. No, if forcing it causes afternoon binge. Listen to true hunger, not habit.

ifThenTemplate.if: If skipping causes overeating at lunch
ifThenTemplate.then: Keep small breakfast — fasting isn't worth binge rebound

whyTemplate: Skipping breakfast extends fasting benefits but only if it doesn't cause dysregulated eating later.

title: When should I prep meals?
answerTemplate: Prep window: evening after dinner or morning before work. Avoid reactive cooking when hungry — decision fatigue causes poor choices. Next meal: {nextMealTime}.
whyTemplate: Meal prep when calm (not hungry) eliminates decision fatigue and preserves meal timing consistency.

title: When can I drink alcohol?
answerTemplate: Minimize or avoid. If drinking: after protein meal, 4+ hours before bed, limit to 1-2 drinks. Alcohol disrupts REM sleep even in small amounts.

ifThenTemplate.then: Skip alcohol — REM disruption costs more than social benefit

whyTemplate: Alcohol fragments sleep architecture and impairs recovery. Even 'moderate' drinking degrades REM quality for 2-3 nights.

title: What to eat before workout?
answerTemplate: For workouts <45min: train fasted or light snack. For workouts >45min: small meal 90min before (protein + simple carbs). Next workout: {nextWorkoutTime}.

nextMovesTemplate.title: If fasted: small protein snack optional

whyTemplate: Fasted training enhances fat oxidation for short sessions. Longer/intense sessions need glycogen — fuel 90min pre for digestion.

title: What to eat after workout?
answerTemplate: Protein + carbs within 90min post-workout. Ratio: 2:1 protein to carbs. Example: chicken + rice, greek yogurt + banana.
whyTemplate: Post-workout is the only time simple carbs are metabolically beneficial — they replenish glycogen without insulin resistance risk.

title: What order should I eat food on my plate?
answerTemplate: Eat in this order: 1) Protein first, 2) Vegetables, 3) Carbs last. This sequence blunts glucose spike by 30-40%.
whyTemplate: Eating protein first slows gastric emptying and signals satiety before you overeat carbs. Simple resequencing, massive metabolic impact.

title: Should I nap?
answerTemplate: Nap only if sleep debt >3h or extreme fatigue. Keep it 20min max, before 3pm. Current time: {nowLocal}. Sleep quality last night: {sleepQuality}/10.

nextMovesTemplate.title: If after 3pm: skip nap, get sunlight instead

ifThenTemplate.then: Fix nighttime sleep first — naps mask the root problem

whyTemplate: Naps reduce sleep pressure for nighttime. Only use as emergency tool, not daily habit.

title: How do I handle eating out?
answerTemplate: Plan ahead: check menu, aim for protein-first meal, stick to meal timing window ({nextMealTime}). Avoid appetizers and refill bread baskets.

ifThenTemplate.then: Enjoy meal, skip alcohol, return to plan next meal — one meal doesn't ruin progress

whyTemplate: Restaurant meals are designed for overconsumption. Strategy: protein focus, portion awareness, meal timing consistency.

title: How do I handle meals while traveling?
answerTemplate: Prioritize: meal timing windows > food quality. Bring protein snacks (jerky, nuts). Stick to your wake/sleep times to preserve circadian rhythm.
whyTemplate: Travel disrupts routine. Preserve timing and sleep schedule — those maintain the system. Food quality can flex temporarily.

title: How do I tell hunger from cravings?
answerTemplate: Hunger: stomach emptiness, gradual onset, flexible food choices. Craving: mouth/brain driven, sudden, specific food fixation. Last meal: {hoursSinceLastMeal}h ago.

nextMovesTemplate.title: Ask: would plain chicken satisfy?
nextMovesTemplate.reason: If yes = hunger; if no = craving

ifThenTemplate.then: Small treat OK if within treat window — fighting it costs more energy than eating it

whyTemplate: Hunger is metabolic need. Cravings are habit, emotion, or hedonic drive. Different problems, different solutions.

title: Do I follow the same schedule on weekends?
answerTemplate: Yes, maintain ±1h of weekday timing. Sleep schedule drift >2h causes 'social jet lag' — metabolic disruption equivalent to flying 2 time zones.

nextMovesTemplate.title: Maintain meal windows
nextMovesTemplate.reason: Insulin sensitivity doesn't take weekends off

nextMovesTemplate.title: Flexibility: food choices, not timing
nextMovesTemplate.reason: When you eat > what you eat

whyTemplate: Circadian rhythm doesn't reset weekly. Weekend schedule drift causes Monday grogginess and metabolic dysfunction.

title: How do I handle shift work?
answerTemplate: Anchor to your wake time: meal 1 within 2h of waking, maintain 4-5h spacing. Avoid eating 2-3h before sleep, regardless of clock time.

nextMovesTemplate.title: Define 'morning' as your wake time
nextMovesTemplate.reason: Circadian rhythm is relative to wake

nextMovesTemplate.title: Fast during your sleep period + 2h before

whyTemplate: Shift work is metabolically hard. Consistency within your shifted schedule is the only lever you control.

title: How should I eat on back-to-back meeting days?
answerTemplate: Use anchor meals: one protein-focused meal before meetings, one compact lunch window, one recovery dinner. Avoid all-day grazing when calendar pressure is high.

ifThenTemplate.then: Use a protein fallback snack and resume normal spacing at dinner

whyTemplate: Meeting-heavy days reduce decision bandwidth. Pre-anchored meals prevent blood sugar swings and late-day overeating.

title: Should I eat before a workout?
answerTemplate: If workout is high intensity or >45min, eat 60-120min before. For light/moderate sessions under 45min, fasted can work if energy is stable.

nextMovesTemplate.title: If light session: hydrate and start

ifThenTemplate.if: If dizzy or weak while fasted
ifThenTemplate.then: Stop and take quick carbs, then reassess session intensity

whyTemplate: Fueling strategy should match session demand. Under-fueling hard workouts raises stress load and can hurt adherence.

title: What do I do if I get hungry late at night?
answerTemplate: First check hydration and stress. If true hunger within 2h of bed, use a small protein-focused snack; avoid high sugar or large meals.

nextMovesTemplate.title: If still hungry: small protein snack
nextMovesTemplate.reason: Supports satiety with lower glucose disruption

ifThenTemplate.then: Increase dinner protein/fiber and review daytime meal spacing

whyTemplate: Late eating can impair sleep, but ignoring true hunger can also fragment sleep. Small, strategic intake is the middle path.
